name: binary-re-dynamic-analysis
description: Use when you need to run a binary, trace execution, or observe runtime behavior. Runtime analysis via QEMU emulation, GDB debugging, and Frida hooking - syscall tracing (strace), breakpoints, memory inspection, function interception. Keywords - "run binary", "execute", "debug", "trace syscalls", "set breakpoint", "qemu", "gdb", "frida", "strace", "watch memory
capabilities: Execute binaries cross-architecture using QEMU user-mode emulation with syscall tracing
Attach GDB debugger to running binaries for instruction-level analysis and breakpoints
Hook function calls with Frida to intercept and inspect runtime arguments
Trace syscalls to observe network connections, file access, and process operations
Run binaries in Docker containers with platform emulation for cross-arch analysis on macOS
Configure sandbox environments with resource limits and network isolation
author: 2389-research
securitySummary: This is a legitimate security research and reverse engineering skill for authorized binary analysis. All static findings are false positives related to documentation examples showing proper use of analysis tools. The skill requires explicit human approval before executing any binaries and emphasizes sandbox isolation. No malicious patterns detected.
valueStatement: Security researchers need to observe actual binary behavior at runtime, not just static code. This skill provides safe, sandboxed execution with QEMU emulation, GDB debugging, and Frida hooking to trace syscalls, inspect memory, and validate reverse engineering hypotheses.
userTitle: Analyze Binaries with Dynamic Runtime Tracing
limitations: Requires explicit human approval before executing any binary for safety
Frida cannot attach to QEMU user-mode targets, requires native architecture or on-device deployment
Some syscalls may be unsupported in QEMU emulation requiring on-device analysis
Anti-analysis techniques in binaries may require additional bypass strategies

useCases.title: Malware Analysis in Sandbox
useCases.description: Security analysts can safely execute suspicious binaries in isolated QEMU environments to observe network behavior, file modifications, and syscall patterns without risking the host system.

useCases.title: Cross-Architecture Firmware Debugging
useCases.description: Embedded systems developers can debug ARM or MIPS binaries on x86 development machines using QEMU emulation with GDB, eliminating the need for physical hardware during analysis.

useCases.title: Reverse Engineering Protected Binaries
useCases.description: Penetration testers can use Frida hooks to intercept cryptographic functions, authentication checks, and network protocols in compiled applications to understand their behavior and find vulnerabilities.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Syscall Trace
promptTemplates.prompt: Use QEMU to run this ARM binary and trace all syscalls to identify what network connections it makes
promptTemplates.scenario: Initial behavior mapping of an unknown binary

promptTemplates.title: Debug with Breakpoints
promptTemplates.prompt: Start this binary under QEMU with GDB attached, set a breakpoint at address 0x8400, and show me the register state when it hits
promptTemplates.scenario: Investigating specific code behavior at known address

promptTemplates.title: Hook Network Functions
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Frida script to intercept all connect() calls in this binary and log the IP addresses and ports it tries to reach

promptTemplates.title: Cross-Platform Container Execution
promptTemplates.prompt: Run this ARM32 binary in a Docker container on macOS with platform emulation and capture all library loading events using LD_DEBUG
promptTemplates.scenario: Advanced cross-architecture analysis with dependency tracing

bestPractices: Always get explicit human approval before executing any binary and document the sandbox configuration being used
Start with QEMU syscall tracing before deeper analysis to understand high-level behavior safely
Use network isolation in sandbox environments when analyzing binaries with unknown network behavior
antiPatterns: Running unknown binaries without sandbox isolation or on production systems
Attempting to use Frida with QEMU user-mode emulation (architecture mismatch will fail)
Using /tmp directory paths for Docker volume mounts on Colima (fails silently, use home directory instead)

faq.question: Can this skill execute Windows PE binaries?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on Linux ELF binaries. For Windows PE analysis, you would need Wine or a Windows VM rather than QEMU user-mode emulation.

faq.question: Is it safe to run malware with this skill?
faq.answer: The skill emphasizes sandbox isolation using QEMU, Docker, or nsjail. However, you must configure appropriate network isolation and get human approval before executing any suspicious binary.

faq.question: Why does Frida not work with QEMU emulated binaries?
faq.answer: Frida requires native architecture execution to inject its agent. QEMU user-mode creates a different process space that Frida cannot attach to. Use on-device frida-server for cross-arch targets.

faq.question: What sandbox configuration should I use for unknown binaries?
faq.answer: Start with QEMU user-mode which provides high isolation. Add network blocking, resource limits, and run as unprivileged user. Document your configuration and get approval before execution.

faq.question: Can I debug ARM binaries on an x86 machine?
faq.answer: Yes, QEMU user-mode emulation allows running ARM binaries on x86 hosts. You can attach gdb-multiarch for debugging. On macOS, use Docker with platform emulation.

faq.question: How do I handle anti-debugging techniques in binaries?
faq.answer: QEMU user-mode bypasses many anti-debug checks like ptrace detection and /proc inspection. For timing checks, use GDB or patch the detection code. The skill provides mitigation strategies in the documentation.
